time_period: Effective from 15 April 2026 (and from mid-June 2026 for further role changes)
body:
• In compliance with UKLR 6.4.9R(2), Admiral announced that Fiona Muldoon (Independent Non-Executive Director) has been appointed an independent Non-Executive Director of Grainger plc with effect from 15 April 2026.
  • Upon appointment, Fiona will join Grainger’s Nomination, Remuneration, Audit and Risk, and Responsible Business Committees.
  • From mid-June 2026, Fiona will become Grainger plc Senior Independent Director and Chair of the Audit and Risk Committee.

time_period: Q3 (to 29 March) and FY ending June 2026 outlook
body:
• Guides to FY total completions of 17,200–17,800 (vs 16,565 in FY2025), implying +3.8% to +7.5% year-on-year.
  • States it remains on track to deliver FY adjusted pretax profit in line with market consensus of £568m, which would be +16% vs £488.3m last year.
  • Upgrades expected year-end net cash to £550m–£650m, described as ~£150m ahead of previous guidance, driven by (i) timing of legacy building remediation payments and (ii) reduced land investment.
  • Says it is being “more selective” on land purchases due to the Middle East crisis and the likely impact on mortgage rates and build cost inflation.
  • Cuts expected FY land approvals to 7,000–9,000 plots (from 10,000–12,000 previously), a reduction of ~25% to ~42% vs the prior range (midpoint -27%).
  • Lowers expected FY land spend to £700m–£800m (from £800m–£900m previously), a reduction of ~11% to ~22% vs the prior range (midpoint -12%).
  • Macro risk framing (company statement): “The ongoing conflict in the Middle East is contributing to increased economic uncertainty, including the potential for a more prolonged higher interest rate environment and renewed cost pressures. While we currently expect any direct impact on FY26 to be limited, visibility beyond the current financial year remains more uncertain.”
  • Q3 net private reservation rate: 0.64 per outlet per week, up from 0.62 (+3.2%).
  • Q3 completions: 3,274, down from 3,717 (-11.9%) due to a strong comparator “ahead of the end of stamp duty relief”.
  • FYTD completions: 10,718 vs 10,824 (-1.0%).
  • Maintains FY build cost inflation guidance of 2%, but flags that higher energy costs are likely to flow into building material costs in FY2027.
  • Re-confirms £100m cost synergy target from the integration of Redrow.

• Agreed a £2.0bn deal to acquire Aegon Europe Holding BV’s UK insurance and pensions operations in a cash and shares transaction.
  • Consideration structure: £750m cash plus issuance of 181.1m shares to Aegon.
  • Cash funding: £650m debt issuance plus existing cash resources.
  • Aegon to become a “new strategic shareholder and asset management partner” with a stake of just over 15%.
  • Aegon lock-up: ends on the earlier of 18 months from completion or completion of Aegon’s holding company redomiciliation to the US (expected start of 2028).
  • Relationship agreement: Aegon can appoint a non-executive director to Standard Life’s board; agreement also “underpins” the strategic asset management partnership.
  • Notes existing strategic shareholders MS&AD Insurance Group Holdings and Aberdeen Group are supportive and remain committed to their stakes.
  • Strategic rationale: accelerates ambition to be “the UK’s leading retirement savings and income business” and further shifts earnings toward “capital-light, pensions and savings”.
  • Post-deal scale: 16m customers and ~£480bn assets under administration.
  • Synergy headline: £800m total net synergy value, including £100m run-rate pretax cost synergies and £340m one-off capital synergies.
  • Financial impact targets: £160m operating cash generation and £190m adjusted operating profit; mid-single digit accretion to adjusted operating EPS by 2029.
  • Timing: subject to regulatory conditions; expected to complete around end-2026.
  • CEO quote (Andy Briggs): “Our agreement to acquire Aegon UK significantly accelerates our vision to be the UK’s leading retirement savings and income business. We will be in an even stronger position to meet the evolving needs of our 16 million customers with enhanced digital, advice and distribution capabilities across Workplace and Retail, strengthening our standing in one of the world’s most attractive markets.”
  • CEO quote (Andy Briggs): “Furthermore, the transaction accelerates our shift to capital-light whilst strengthening our cash, capital and earnings position to create increased value for shareholders.”

• Estimated Q1 copper production 143,000t, down 19% QoQ (vs 177,000t in Q4 2025) and down 7.6% YoY (vs 154,700t).
  • Attributes lower copper production to “lower processing rates, and lower copper grades”; says mine plan remains on track at Los Pelambres and Centinela concentrators.
  • Copper sales 137,000t, down 32% QoQ (vs 201,000t) and down 20% YoY (vs 170,200t).
  • Gold production 46,500oz, down from 66,300oz QoQ (-29.9%), but +8.4% YoY, reflecting higher gold grades partly offset by lower ore processing rates at Los Pelambres and Centinela.
  • Molybdenum output 3,000t, down from 4,400t QoQ (-31.8%) and down from 3,100t YoY (-3.2%), reflecting a mix of recoveries and processing rates.
  • Cash costs before by-product credits $2.77 per pound, +17% YoY, driven by higher costs at the two concentrators.
  • By-product credits $1.69 per pound, “more than doubled” YoY on stronger gold and molybdenum pricing, resulting in net cash costs 30% lower YoY vs $1.54 last year (implied ~$1.08).
  • Maintains full-year copper production guidance of 650,000–700,000t.
  • Keeps cash cost targets unchanged (ranges as stated), assuming fuel prices return to January 2026 levels during Q2, normalising from the spike linked to Middle East conflict and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
  • Reiterates annual capex guidance of $3.4bn.
  • CEO quote (Ivan Arriagada): “As we move through the year, we expect copper production to increase quarter-on-quarter, supported by higher ore processing rates and improving grades at Los Pelambres, in line with the mine plan.”
  • CEO quote (Ivan Arriagada): “Pre-commissioning activities are now underway at the Centinela Second concentrator project, while progress across the Los Pelambres growth enabling projects continues to strengthen the operational platform for future production growth, as we look to increase our copper production by 30%.”
  • CEO quote (Ivan Arriagada): “Against a backdrop of higher energy prices, we remain focused on safety, operational excellence, disciplined cost control and the timely execution of our growth and development projects, while maintaining resilient supply chains, and to date we have experienced no supply disruptions. The copper price remains constructive in 2026, and the medium-term fundamentals for copper are compelling, underpinned by structural demand drivers and constrained supply.”

• Director declaration under UK Listing Rule 6.4.6: Natasja Laheij (ASOS Chair) appointed to the Supervisory Board of PostNL N.V. with effect from 14 April 2026.
  • Natasja Laheij will also serve as Chair of PostNL’s Audit Committee.

time_period: Board changes effective July 2026 and September 2026
body:
• Appoints Therese Esperdy as new Chair, effective 1 September 2026.
  • Esperdy to join the board 1 July 2026 as non-executive director and chair designate.
  • Esperdy is currently non-executive chair at Imperial Brands PLC (with a forthcoming retirement noted) and holds other roles including Smith & Nephew (senior independent director designate) and Moody’s Corp (non-executive director).
  • Background context: Rentokil previously announced (March 5) that outgoing Chair Richard Solomons intended to step down.
  • Outgoing Chair quote (Richard Solomons): “It has been an immense privilege to serve as chair of Rentokil Initial over what has been a period of growth and transformation for the business. With the successful appointment of Mike Duffy as CEO, and a clear strategy developed for North American growth, 2026 is the right time for me to step down and ensure a smooth handover to Therese. I have great confidence in the future success of Rentokil Initial — it’s a high-quality business, full of exceptional people, operating in highly resilient growth markets.”

• Reported revenue £141.5m, up 9.5% year-on-year.
  • Like-for-like revenue growth +1.9%.
  • UK revenue £118.4m, up 9.4% year-on-year.
  • UK like-for-like revenue growth +2.6%.
  • Canada revenue CAD 42.9m, up 13% year-on-year (in CAD, to strip FX); Canada like-for-like revenue +0.5% (in CAD).
  • States high gross margins mean it is “well-insulated against inflationary pressures.”
  • Balance sheet: net cash £26.0m with an undrawn £25m revolving credit facility.
  • Interim results date: 27 May.
  • CEO quote (Stephen Burns): “The benefits of the investments made throughout the UK and Canadian estate, combined with proactive demand generation initiatives and disciplined cost management, are reflected in our strong first-half performance. Demand for high-quality, family leisure activities that offer great value for money also remains resilient in both territories, and our cash generative business model allows us to invest where we see opportunities and deliver profitable growth.”

time_period: Q4 (three months ended March) and FY2026
body:
• Q4: signed 28 leases and renewals, representing £23.8m GPE share of annual rent.
  • Q4: market lettings estimated ~16% ahead of March 2025 ERV.
  • Pre-letting: The Delft (formerly Minerva House) pre-let to Quantexa on a 10-year lease.
  • Let the final office space at SIX St Andrew Street to an AI company; building now fully let and expected to generate £8.8m annual rent.
  • SIX St Andrew Street economics: £8.8m annual rent is 6.2% ahead of ERV and implies a 6.2% yield on cost.
  • Q4 disposals: two property disposals with headline values £172m and £52m.
  • FY2026 disposals: £490m total; stated as ~2% ahead of March 2025 book values.
  • Q4 rent reviews: settled 7 rent reviews, +49% vs previous passing rent (includes leases with KKR and Glencore).
  • FY2026 leasing: 88 leases and renewals representing £69.6m GPE share of annual rent.
  • FY2026: market lettings 10% higher than ERV (vs a year prior).
  • Currently under offer: ~£6.4m of rent.
  • CEO quote (Toby Courtauld): “Despite a volatile macroeconomic backdrop, this has been an excellent finish to the year…reflecting the strength of demand for high quality, well located space and the momentum in our fully managed offer. Looking forward, we have further leasing under offer and a strong pipeline of new space in production, and so we start the new financial year with positive momentum.”

Time Period Effective from 15 April 2026 (and from mid-June 2026 for further role changes)

• In compliance with UKLR 6.4.9R(2), Admiral announced that Fiona Muldoon (Independent Non-Executive Director) has been appointed an independent Non-Executive Director of Grainger plc with effect from 15 April 2026.

• Upon appointment, Fiona will join Grainger’s Nomination, Remuneration, Audit and Risk, and Responsible Business Committees.

• From mid-June 2026, Fiona will become Grainger plc Senior Independent Director and Chair of the Audit and Risk Committee.

Barratt Redrow PLC

Publish Date Not stated (article says “on Wednesday”)

Time Period Q3 (to 29 March) and FY ending June 2026 outlook

• Guides to FY total completions of 17,200–17,800 (vs 16,565 in FY2025), implying +3.8% to +7.5% year-on-year.

• States it remains on track to deliver FY adjusted pretax profit in line with market consensus of £568m, which would be +16% vs £488.3m last year.

• Upgrades expected year-end net cash to £550m–£650m, described as ~£150m ahead of previous guidance, driven by (i) timing of legacy building remediation payments and (ii) reduced land investment.

• Says it is being “mores selective” on land purchases due to the Middle East crisis and the likely impact on mortgage rates and build cost inflation.

• Cuts expected FY land approvals to 7,000–9,000 plots (from 10,000–12,000 previously), a reduction of ~25% to ~42% vs the prior range (midpoint -27%).

• Lowers expected FY land spend to £700m–£800m (from £800m–£900m previously), a reduction of ~11% to ~22% vs the prior range (midpoint -12%).

• Macro risk framing (company statement): “The ongoing conflict in the Middle East is contributing to increased economic uncertainty, including the potential for a more prolonged higher interest rate environment and renewed cost pressures. While we currently expect any direct impact on FY26 to be limited, visibility beyond the current financial year remains more uncertain,”.

• Q3 net private reservation rate: 0.64 per outlet per week, up from 0.62 (+3.2%).

• Q3 completions: 3,274, down from 3,717 (-11.9%) due to a strong comparator “ahead of the end of stamp duty relief”.

• FYTD completions: 10,718 vs 10,824 (-1.0%).

• Maintains FY build cost inflation guidance of 2%, but flags that higher energy costs are likely to flow into building material costs in FY2027.

• Re-confirms £100m cost synergy target from the integration of Redrow.

Standard Life PLC

Publish Date Not stated (article says “on Wednesday”)

Time Period Transaction announcement (expected completion around end-2026)

• Agreed a £2.0bn deal to acquire Aegon Europe Holding BV’s UK insurance and pensions operations in a cash and shares transaction.

• Consideration structure: £750m cash plus issuance of 181.1m shares to Aegon.

• Cash funding: £650m debt issuance plus existing cash resources.

• Aegon to become a “new strategic shareholder and asset management partner” with a stake of just over 15%.

• Aegon lock-up: ends on the earlier of 18 months from completion or completion of Aegon’s holding company redomiciliation to the US (expected start of 2028).

• Relationship agreement: Aegon can appoint a non-executive director to Standard Life’s board; agreement also “underpins” the strategic asset management partnership.

• Notes existing strategic shareholders MS&AD Insurance Group Holdings and Aberdeen Group are supportive and remain committed to their stakes.

• Strategic rationale: accelerates ambition to be “the UK's leading retirement savings and income business” and further shifts earnings toward “capital-light, pensions and savings”.

• Post-deal scale: 16m customers and ~£480bn assets under administration.

• Synergy headline: £800m total net synergy value, including £100m run-rate pretax cost synergies and £340m one-off capital synergies.

• Financial impact targets: £160m operating cash generation and £190m adjusted operating profit; mid-single digit accretion to adjusted operating EPS by 2029.

• Timing: subject to regulatory conditions; expected to complete around end-2026.

• CEO quote (Andy Briggs): “Our agreement to acquire Aegon UK significantly accelerates our vision to be the UK's leading retirement savings and income business. We will be in an even stronger position to meet the evolving needs of our 16 million customers with enhanced digital, advice and distribution capabilities across Workplace and Retail, strengthening our standing in one of the world's most attractive markets,”.

• CEO quote (Andy Briggs): “Furthermore, the transaction accelerates our shift to capital-light whilst strengthening our cash, capital and earnings position to create increased value for shareholders.”

Antofagasta PLC

Publish Date Not stated (article references Monday report; shares noted on Wednesday)

Time Period Q1 2026

• Estimated Q1 copper production 143,000t, down 19% QoQ (vs 177,000t in Q4 2025) and down 7.6% YoY (vs 154,700t).

• Attributes lower copper production to “lower processing rates, and lower copper grades”; says mine plan remains on track at Los Pelambres and Centinela concentrators.

• Copper sales 137,000t, down 32% QoQ (vs 201,000t) and down 20% YoY (vs 170,200t).

• Gold production 46,500oz, down from 66,300oz QoQ (-29.9%), but +8.4% YoY, reflecting higher gold grades partly offset by lower ore processing rates at Los Pelambres and Centinela.

• Molybdenum output 3,000t, down from 4,400t QoQ (-31.8%) and down from 3,100t YoY (-3.2%), reflecting a mix of recoveries and processing rates.

• Cash costs before by-product credits 2.77 per pound, +17% YoY, driven by higher costs at the two concentrators.

• By-product credits 1.69 per pound, “more than doubled” YoY on stronger gold and molybdenum pricing, resulting in net cash costs 30% lower YoY vs 1.54 last year (implied ~1.08).

• Maintains full-year copper production guidance of 650,000–700,000t.

• Keeps cash cost targets unchanged (ranges as stated), assuming fuel prices return to January 2026 levels during Q2, normalising from the spike linked to Middle East conflict and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

• Reiterates annual capex guidance of $3.4bn.

• CEO quote (Ivan Arriagada): “As we move through the year, we expect copper production to increase quarter-on-quarter, supported by higher ore processing rates and improving grades at Los Pelambres, in line with the mine plan.”

• CEO quote (Ivan Arriagada): “Pre-commissioning activities are now underway at the Centinela Second concentrator project, while progress across the Los Pelambres growth enabling projects continues to strengthen the operational platform for future production growth, as we look to increase our copper production by 30%.”

• CEO quote (Ivan Arriagada): “Against a backdrop of higher energy prices, we remain focused on safety, operational excellence, disciplined cost control and the timely execution of our growth and development projects, while maintaining resilient supply chains, and to date we have experienced no supply disruptions. The copper price remains constructive in 2026, and the medium-term fundamentals for copper are compelling, underpinned by structural demand drivers and constrained supply.”

ASOS PLC

Publish Date 15 April 2026

Time Period Effective from 14 April 2026

• Director declaration under UK Listing Rule 6.4.6: Natasja Laheij (ASOS Chair) appointed to the Supervisory Board of PostNL N.V. with effect from 14 April 2026.

• Natasja Laheij will also serve as Chair of PostNL’s Audit Committee.

summary: Hunting backs FY2026 EBITDA guidance of USD145-155m despite Q1 decline
body:
Time Period First quarter 2026 (Q1); FY2026 outlook

  • Backed full-year EBITDA guidance of USD145m–USD155m.
  • Reiterated earnings will be second-half weighted, projecting a 40/60 split in earnings between H1/H2 due to order execution and delivery timings.
  • Said Q1 trading was in line with expectations, with subsea contract milestones weighted to Q2.
  • Reported Q1 EBITDA of USD23.2m, down 40% from USD38.7m a year ago.
  • Q1 EBITDA margin 10%, narrowed from 14% a year ago (-4pp).
  • Stated: "All product groups have traded as expected, with Perforating Systems' sales in North America reporting a performance ahead of expectations as higher quality sales and production efficiencies continue to improve trading results,".
  • Order book USD428.8m (Tuesday), up 20% vs USD358.0m at end-December.
  • Flagged some slippage in Middle East tender issuance since the outbreak of war in March; expects tenders to be published in Q2.
  • Commented on Middle East exposure: "Hunting's operations and personnel in Dubai and Saudi Arabia remain safe and generally unaffected by the Middle East conflict. At this point management expects minimal impact on current year profitability, although this position remains predicated on the tenure of the conflict,".
  • Noted higher commodity prices have not yet translated into higher US or international rig counts, but said it is well placed to benefit if activity rises.
  • USD15m cost reduction programme is accelerating; majority of savings expected by early 2028.
  • Shared service functions in Europe and North America expected to be fully operational by mid-2026.
  • Plans to combine EMEA and Asia Pacific operating segments into a single International segment from the start of 2027 to drive further cost efficiencies.
  • Continues to assess bolt-on acquisitions; pipeline under review with a particular focus on subsea businesses.

summary: Segro European Logistics Partnership JV prices EUR500m 5-year bond at 3.875%
body:
Time Period Bond issuance announcement (SEGRO European Logistics Partnership JV)

  • As venture adviser to the Segro European Logistics Partnership JV, reported pricing of a EUR500m senior unsecured bond.
  • Bond term: 5 years.
  • Priced at 105bp above euro mid-swaps.
  • Implied annual coupon: 3.875%.
  • Orderbook strength: bond issue was over 10x covered at peak.

summary: Tesco expects flat operating profit despite 14% revenue growth amid retail margin pressures
body:
Time Period FY to 26 February 2026 (expected results); outlook into FY2026/27

  • Expected (company-compiled consensus) revenue ex-VAT incl fuel: GBP72.55bn, up from GBP63.64bn (+GBP8.91bn; +14.0%).
  • Expected consensus group adjusted operating profit: GBP3.10bn, down from GBP3.13bn (-1.0%).
  • Reminder from January guidance: expects FY2026 group adjusted operating profit at the upper end of GBP2.9bn–GBP3.1bn range.
  • Continues to expect retail free cash flow within medium-term guidance range of GBP1.4bn–GBP1.8bn.
  • Share context given: shares +0.9% to 474.30p on Wednesday afternoon; +37% over the past year; “largely flat” since onset of the Middle East conflict (per the article).
  • Market position noted: market share “around 28%” amid competition from Aldi/Lidl and a “renewed Asda”.
  • Citi (Monique Pollard) expectation: adjusted operating profit GBP3.08bn, assuming GBP500m cost savings, and retail free cash flow GBP1.77bn.
  • Citi Q4 view: UK like-for-like growth 3.4% vs 3.1% Visible Alpha consensus (as cited).
  • Jefferies forecast: sales GBP72.28bn, adjusted operating profit GBP3.13bn, EPS 28.8p; expects a “strong end to 25/26 and resilient margin performance”.
  • Jefferies capex/FCF angle: “4.5% below cons FCF” in 26/27 as capex grows to GBP1.6bn.
  • Weather/comps watchpoint: “tough comparisons” in Q1; analysts watching if wet weather has dampened sales.
  • Michael Hewson on the margin/outlook risk: "If things weren't already tough for retailers when it comes to margins, the outlook appears set to get even worse, with the effects of the conflict in the Middle East set to muddy the outlook further. Higher employment costs, along with higher fuel prices are likely to exert upward pressure on margins as look towards the new fiscal year... Guidance is likely to be key here, with all UK food retailers having to bear the costs of higher energy prices, labour costs, as well as increased competition as Aldi and Lidl continue to open new stores."
  • AJ Bell on inflation dynamics and competitive stance: "Tesco's defensive credentials have seen it in demand even during the recent market turbulence, with the weekly shop being a non-negotiable for most people. However, investors will be looking for an update on how renewed inflationary pressures might impact the business... Inflation should nominally boost sales figures and translate into increased revenue, but volumes may be affected as consumers cut back and margins, which are always thin in the supermarket game, will come under pressure. A key question will be the extent to which Tesco seeks to take on some pain in order to protect its competitive position."

summary: Ferrexpo's Q1 production plummeted 72% due to Ukrainian power infrastructure attacks
body:
Time Period First quarter 2026 (Q1)

  • Reported Q1 total commercial production 592,751 tonnes, down 72% from 2.1m tonnes a year earlier.
  • Q1 output split: premium iron ore concentrate 67,825 tonnes, down 91% year-on-year.
  • Q1 output split: premium iron ore pellets 524,926 tonnes, down 61% year-on-year.
  • Said production was “largely suspended” during Q1 due to “nationwide attacks on Ukrainian electricity generation and transmission infrastructure”.
  • Operations restarted at reduced capacity at the end of February, citing improved electricity availability and pricing.
  • Financial risk framing: continuing to monitor cash and working capital, and actively exploring funding options such as an equity raise.
  • Flagged VAT risk: if withholding of VAT refunds persists and funding issues aren’t resolved, “this could give rise to material negative consequences for the group”.
  • Interim Executive Chair Lucio Genovese on suspension decision: "The lower production numbers in the first quarter, at almost half of what we achieved in the final three months of 2025, reflect focused Russian attacks on Ukraine's power and energy infrastructure late last year and the resultant impact on our ability to operate sustainably. By January, given the supply of electricity could not be assured on a sustainable basis in the volumes required, we were forced to make the difficult decision to temporarily suspend operations and place a part of the workforce on furlough,"
  • Genovese on priorities: "Going forward, our focus remains on managing working capital and costs throughout this challenging operating environment."
  • Market reaction noted: shares down 2.9% to 43.62p on Wednesday afternoon.

summary: CME expands dividend index derivatives suite with mid-curve options and new quarterly futures
body:
Time Period Product expansion announcement; planned launch 11 May 2026 (pending regulatory review)

  • Announced plans to expand its Equity Index Dividend suite on 11 May 2026, pending regulatory review.
  • Adding mid-curve options on S&P 500 Annual Dividend Index futures.
  • Adding Nasdaq-100 Quarterly Dividend Index futures.
  • Adding Russell 2000 Quarterly Dividend Index futures.
  • Joe Hickey (Global Head of Equity Products) on demand + rationale: "Amid shifting interest rates and economic uncertainty, managing dividend exposure has become a strategic necessity for investors... Trading in our dividend products has increased more than 50% year-over-year. To meet this increased demand, we are launching these new Mid-curve options on S&P 500 Annual Index futures and Quarterly Dividend futures on the Nasdaq-100 and Russell 2000 to provide clients with additional, and even more precise tools to mitigate index-specific risk and hedge around critical dividend events."
  • Stated open interest across dividend futures/options: 860,000 contracts, up 27% year-over-year.
  • Stated S&P 500 Quarterly Dividend Index futures average daily volume up 21% year-over-year.
  • Stated options on S&P Annual Dividend Index futures ADV 3,900 contracts, up 80% year-over-year.
  • Confirmed products will be listed on and subject to CME rules.
